result:
I keep thinking about the moment just before a wave breaks.

Not the crash itself—that’s loud, obvious, everybody’s filming it on their phones. I mean the hush right before, when the water sucks back and exposes a brief mirror of wet sand. In that sucked-back second the whole shoreline holds its breath. Shells, bottle caps, the ankle bone of some long-dead dog—all of it glistens like artifacts on a museum tray. Then the mirror tilts, the lip folds, and the moment is erased under a ton of translucent green.

I think civilization is living inside that inhale. We keep building sandcastles during the exposed-mirror part, pretending the mirror will stay. We write constitutions, store data in redundant bunkers under mountains, invent words like “forever” and “backup.” Meanwhile the ocean is already leaning forward again, sun lighting its face from inside like a lantern.

Yesterday I watched an algorithm try to finish a Beethoven symphony. It produced something that sounded like Beethoven on SSRIs—technically plush, emotionally laminated. The comments underneath were ecstatic: “Finally, we can have new Beethoven whenever we want!” I felt the same vertigo I feel when I see condo ads erected on dunes: an empire of souvenir shops at the edge of an eroding spit.

My nephew is seven. He asked why old people aren’t scared of thunder. I told him it’s because by the time you’re old you’ve heard so much thunder you’ve started to sympathize with the storm. He considered this, then offered that maybe storms are just dinosaurs upstairs moving furniture. I like his version better; it leaves room for negotiation.

I keep a notebook of obsolete futures: flying postmen, smell-o-vision, the Soviet plan to reverse the flow of Siberian rivers. Page 47 is glued to a foil yogurt lid labeled “TASTE OF 2000.” The yogurt inside evaporated decades ago, but the foil still insists on the promise. I flip to that page when people say the future will be better because it’s technically feasible. Feasibility is a drunk chauffeur; destination seldom matters to him.

There’s a small crack forming in the plaster above my desk. At night it looks like the coastline of an unnamed continent. I trace it with my finger and imagine tiny colonists landing on its shores, planting flags, arguing about property rights. By morning the crack has crept a millimeter. One day the entire ceiling will calve like arctic ice, but tonight it’s just a dark thread, a proposition.

Maybe that’s enough: to keep describing the thread until the lantern light reaches us, to pass the notebook of obsolete futures to the next kid who learns how to read thunder.
